enrichment.short_description: Open-source backup software that creates encrypted, incremental backups to cloud and on-premises storage.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Duplicati is open-source backup software offering AES-256 encrypted, incremental backups to cloud and on-prem storage, with free and paid tiers.

features.title: Bring Your Own Storage
features.description: Back up to a wide range of cloud providers (AWS S3, Azure, Google Cloud, Dropbox, OneDrive, Backblaze) or on-premises targets like NAS, SFTP, and SMB.

features.title: Zero-trust encryption
features.description: Data is encrypted with AES-256 before it leaves the device, so storage providers never see unencrypted data.

features.title: Block-level incremental backups
features.description: Only changed data blocks are transferred after the initial backup, reducing bandwidth and storage use.

features.title: Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace protection
features.description: Platform-specific backup support for Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace data.

features.title: Multi-directional backup
features.description: Supports on-prem-to-cloud, cloud-to-on-prem, and local-to-local backup configurations.

features.title: Centralized management (paid tiers)
features.description: Pro and Enterprise plans add remote management, alerts, and multi-site deployment across many endpoints.

pros: Free tier available for up to 2 machines
Open-source with AES-256 client-side encryption
Wide range of supported cloud and on-prem storage backends
Block-level deduplication reduces bandwidth usage
cons: Free tier is limited to 2 machines and a capped backup history
Paid Pro/Enterprise tiers required for centralized management and remote monitoring
Enterprise pricing requires a 10-machine minimum

faqs.question: Is Duplicati free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Duplicati offers a free tier for up to 2 machines with a limited backup history. Paid Pro and Enterprise plans remove these limits and add management features.

faqs.question: How does Duplicati protect my data?
faqs.answer: Duplicati encrypts data with AES-256 before it leaves your device, so cloud storage providers never see unencrypted backups.

faqs.question: What storage services does Duplicati support?
faqs.answer: Duplicati supports major cloud providers including AWS S3, Azure, Google Cloud, Dropbox, OneDrive, and Backblaze, as well as on-premises NAS, SFTP, and SMB targets.

faqs.question: Can Duplicati back up Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace?
faqs.answer: Yes, Duplicati includes platform-specific backup support for Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace.

use_cases.title: Personal encrypted cloud backup
use_cases.description: Individuals backing up personal files to their own cloud storage account with client-side encryption.

use_cases.title: SMB endpoint backup
use_cases.description: Small and medium businesses backing up multiple machines to cloud or on-prem storage with centralized oversight.

use_cases.title: MSP-managed backup
use_cases.description: Managed service providers deploying and monitoring backups silently across many client endpoints.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Duplicati?
paragraphs: Duplicati is an open-source backup tool that creates encrypted, incremental backups of files and sends them to cloud storage or on-premises destinations. Backups are encrypted with AES-256 before leaving the source device.
The project offers a free tier for small setups, alongside paid Pro and Enterprise plans that add centralized management, alerting, and support for larger fleets of machines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Duplicati is for
paragraphs: Duplicati suits individuals who want encrypted backups to their own cloud storage, as well as small businesses and MSPs that need to manage backups across multiple endpoints using the paid tiers.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source client/server backup system combining image and file backups for fast restores.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: UrBackup is a free, open-source AGPLv3 backup system combining file and image backups with deduplication and bare-metal restore.

features.title: Combined image and file backups
features.description: Performs both file-level and disk-image backups for comprehensive data protection and fast restoration.

features.title: Continuous change monitoring
features.description: Monitors folders for changes so subsequent incremental backups run quickly.

features.title: Web-based management
features.description: A web interface handles server configuration, client status monitoring, and file recovery.

features.title: Bare-metal restore
features.description: Supports bootable USB restoration of full disk images.

features.title: Client-side deduplication
features.description: Files identical across multiple clients are stored only once to reduce storage use.

features.title: Internet-capable backup
features.description: Can back up remote or mobile clients over the internet, not just the local network.

pros: Completely free and open source under AGPLv3, usable commercially
Combines image and file backup in one tool
Deduplication reduces storage requirements across many clients
Can back up open files (e.g., Outlook .pst) without interrupting them
cons: No official paid support tier for enterprises needing SLAs
Web interface and setup are less polished than commercial competitors

faqs.question: Is UrBackup free?
faqs.answer: Yes, UrBackup is completely free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, for both personal and commercial use.

faqs.question: What platforms does UrBackup support?
faqs.answer: The server runs on Windows, GNU/Linux, FreeBSD, and NAS operating systems; clients are available for Windows, macOS, and GNU/Linux.

faqs.question: Does UrBackup support bare-metal restore?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports restoring a full disk image from a bootable USB stick.

faqs.question: How does UrBackup save storage space?
faqs.answer: It deduplicates identical files across multiple clients so they are stored only once on the server.

use_cases.title: Small office network backup
use_cases.description: Backing up file servers and workstations on a local network with both file and image backups.

use_cases.title: Remote/mobile client backup
use_cases.description: Backing up laptops and remote machines over the internet outside the local network.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is UrBackup?
paragraphs: UrBackup is a free, open-source client/server backup system that combines file and image backups to deliver both data safety and fast restoration. It monitors folders continuously so incremental backups run quickly after the initial full backup.
The server offers a web-based interface for configuration, client monitoring, and file recovery, and supports bare-metal restore from a bootable USB stick.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who UrBackup is for
paragraphs: UrBackup fits organizations and individuals who want a no-cost, self-hosted backup system covering both file-level and full disk-image backups across Windows, macOS, and Linux clients.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, AGPLv3-licensed enterprise backup and recovery software with optional paid support subscriptions.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bareos is open-source AGPLv3 enterprise backup software with hybrid cloud/on-prem backup and optional paid support subscriptions.

features.title: Ransomware protection
features.description: Includes security capabilities aimed at protecting backups from ransomware.

features.title: Hybrid cloud and on-premise backup
features.description: Supports backup scenarios spanning cloud and on-premises infrastructure.

features.title: Cross-platform, exabyte-scale support
features.description: Runs across Linux, Windows, macOS, FreeBSD, and ARM64, scaling into the exabyte range.

features.title: Encrypted communications
features.description: Supports encrypted client-server communication and client-side encryption.

features.title: Certified platform support
features.description: Officially certified for SUSE Linux Enterprise Server, SUSE Enterprise Storage, and Red Hat Enterprise Linux.

features.title: Professional support and training
features.description: Paid subscription tiers add tested binary packages, defined-response-time support, and training courses.

pros: Open source under AGPLv3 with no license fees
Certified for SUSE and Red Hat enterprise platforms
Scales to exabyte-range environments
Used by universities, public institutions, and enterprises such as SUSE and Amnesty International
cons: Tested/maintained binary packages require a paid subscription
Support tiers are priced in a unit-based model that can be complex to calculate
Higher support tiers (Premier, Global) are expensive (up to €42,900/year)

faqs.question: Is Bareos free?
faqs.answer: Bareos is open source under AGPLv3 with no license fees. A paid annual subscription (starting at €480/year) provides tested binary packages and access to support.

faqs.question: Who maintains Bareos?
faqs.answer: Bareos is developed by the Bareos community and Bareos GmbH & Co. KG, founded in 2012.

faqs.question: What platforms does Bareos support?
faqs.answer: Bareos supports Linux, Windows, macOS, FreeBSD, and ARM64.

faqs.question: Does Bareos offer training?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bareos GmbH & Co. KG offers training courses, starting with the Admin I course from €1,350.

use_cases.title: Enterprise data protection
use_cases.description: Backing up hybrid cloud and on-premises infrastructure at scale with certified platform support.

use_cases.title: University and research backup
use_cases.description: Managing large-scale backup and recovery for academic and research computing environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Bareos?
paragraphs: Bareos is an open-source, AGPLv3-licensed backup and recovery solution built for enterprise environments, offering hybrid cloud and on-premises backup with encrypted communications and ransomware-protection features. The project has operated since 2012.
While the software itself carries no license fees, Bareos GmbH & Co. KG offers paid subscriptions for tested binary packages plus tiered support contracts, training, and consulting services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Bareos is for
paragraphs: Bareos is used by universities, public institutions, service providers, and enterprises — including SUSE, the University of Utah, and Amnesty International — that need certified, scalable backup infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, modular backup and recovery system for networks, with a paid Enterprise Edition from Bacula Systems.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bacula is open-source, modular backup and recovery software for Linux, Windows, and Mac, with a paid Enterprise Edition for larger deployments.

features.title: TLS-encrypted communications
features.description: Automatic encryption of communications between Bacula components.

features.title: Kubernetes and Docker plugins
features.description: Backs up containerized workloads with dedicated Kubernetes and Docker plugins.

features.title: Multi-tier backup jobs
features.description: Supports incremental, differential, and synthetic backups along with migration and copy jobs.

features.title: Database and hypervisor plugins
features.description: Plugins for databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server) and hypervisors (VMware, Hyper-V, KVM, XCP-ng).

features.title: Cloud storage support
features.description: Backs up to cloud platforms including AWS and OpenStack.

features.title: LDAP console authentication
features.description: Supports external LDAP authentication for the management console.

pros: Community Edition is free and open source
Broad plugin ecosystem covering Kubernetes, Docker, databases, and hypervisors
Supports tape drives and autochangers for large-scale archival
Reports 2.5 million downloads via SourceForge
cons: Advanced scalability and security features are reserved for the paid Enterprise Edition
Community Edition support relies on community forums, not commercial SLAs
Configuration is text/console-based and has a learning curve

faqs.question: Is Bacula free?
faqs.answer: The Bacula Community Edition is free and open source. Bacula Systems also sells a commercial Enterprise Edition with additional scalability, security features, and paid support.

faqs.question: What platforms does Bacula support?
faqs.answer: Bacula supports Linux, Windows, and Mac, plus Kubernetes environments, virtual machines (VMware, Hyper-V, KVM, XCP-ng), and tape drives/autochangers.

faqs.question: Can Bacula back up databases?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes plugins for databases such as MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQL Server.

faqs.question: Does Bacula support cloud storage?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bacula supports cloud platforms including AWS and OpenStack.

use_cases.title: Mixed-OS network backup
use_cases.description: Backing up heterogeneous networks of Linux, Windows, and Mac systems from a central console.

use_cases.title: Containerized workload backup
use_cases.description: Protecting Kubernetes and Docker workloads using dedicated plugins.

use_cases.title: Enterprise-scale backup
use_cases.description: Organizations needing tape archival, database plugins, and commercial support via the Enterprise Edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Bacula?
paragraphs: Bacula is an open-source, modular backup and recovery system for managing backup, recovery, and verification of data across networks of computers running different operating systems. It supports incremental, differential, and synthetic backups, plus plugins for Kubernetes, Docker, hypervisors, and major databases.
The free Community Edition is maintained by the Bacula open-source community, while Bacula Systems sells a commercial Enterprise Edition with added scalability, security, and support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Bacula is for
paragraphs: Bacula suits system administrators and enterprises that need a flexible, plugin-extensible backup system spanning physical servers, virtual machines, containers, and tape archival.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source enterprise backup solution for VMs, containers, and physical hosts, with optional paid support subscriptions.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Proxmox Backup Server is open-source enterprise backup software for VMs, containers, and physical hosts, with tiered paid support subscriptions.

features.title: Incremental, deduplicated backups
features.description: Reduces network load and storage consumption through incremental, fully deduplicated backups.

features.title: Strong encryption and integrity checks
features.description: Backups are encrypted with data integrity verification built in.

features.title: Web-based GUI
features.description: Centralized management and monitoring through a web interface.

features.title: Tight Proxmox VE integration
features.description: Integrates closely with Proxmox Virtual Environment for backing up VMs and containers.

features.title: Namespace and sync jobs
features.description: Supports namespaces and sync jobs for organizing and replicating backup data.

features.title: Tape backup support
features.description: Supports LTO tape backup for long-term archival.

pros: Full open-source feature set available for free (Community tier)
Deduplication and incremental backups reduce storage and network overhead
Tight integration with Proxmox Virtual Environment
Support for tape (LTO) archival
cons: Paid subscriptions required for enterprise repository access and guaranteed support response times
Higher support tiers (Premium at €4,480/year) are costly for smaller teams
Best suited to environments already using Proxmox VE

faqs.question: Is Proxmox Backup Server free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the complete feature set is available for free under the Community subscription, which includes stable updates but only community support.

faqs.question: What do paid Proxmox Backup Server subscriptions add?
faqs.answer: Paid Basic, Standard, and Premium tiers add access to the enterprise repository and guaranteed support ticket response times, from 1 business day down to 2 hours.

faqs.question: Does Proxmox Backup Server work with virtual machines?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates tightly with Proxmox Virtual Environment to back up VMs, containers, and physical hosts.

faqs.question: Does Proxmox Backup Server support tape backup?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports LTO tape backup for archival.

use_cases.title: Proxmox VE backup
use_cases.description: Backing up VMs and containers running on Proxmox Virtual Environment.

use_cases.title: Datacenter backup
use_cases.description: Centralized, deduplicated backup for datacenter infrastructure with optional enterprise support.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Proxmox Backup Server?
paragraphs: Proxmox Backup Server is an open-source enterprise backup solution for backing up and restoring virtual machines, containers, and physical hosts, built by Proxmox Server Solutions GmbH (founded 2004). It performs incremental, fully deduplicated backups and integrates tightly with Proxmox Virtual Environment.

The complete feature set is available for free under the Community subscription; paid Basic, Standard, and Premium tiers add access to the enterprise update repository and guaranteed support response times.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Proxmox Backup Server is for
paragraphs: It's built for datacenter operators and IT administrators, particularly those already running Proxmox Virtual Environment, who want an open-source alternative to commercial backup systems.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source continuous file synchronization tool that syncs files directly between devices without central servers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Syncthing is free, open-source continuous file synchronization software (MPLv2) that syncs files directly between devices with no central server.

features.title: Peer-to-peer synchronization
features.description: Files sync directly between devices with no central server storing your data.

features.title: TLS encryption with perfect forward secrecy
features.description: All communication between devices is encrypted.

features.title: Certificate-based device authentication
features.description: Devices are authenticated using cryptographic certificates rather than passwords.

features.title: Open, published protocol
features.description: Uses an open protocol with published specifications rather than a proprietary format.

features.title: Web-based interface
features.description: Configuration and monitoring are done through a browser-based UI.

features.title: Multi-folder sync
features.description: Supports syncing multiple folders with different sharing arrangements across devices.

pros: Free and open source under MPLv2
No central server means data stays under user control
Encrypted, authenticated device-to-device connections
Runs on many platforms including Linux, macOS, Windows, FreeBSD, Solaris, and OpenBSD
cons: No official first-party commercial support (third-party support available via Kastelo)
Requires all synced devices to be online simultaneously for changes to propagate directly
Initial setup and device pairing is more technical than mainstream cloud-sync apps

faqs.question: Is Syncthing free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Syncthing is free and open source, licensed under MPLv2.

faqs.question: Does Syncthing use a central server?
faqs.answer: No, Syncthing synchronizes files directly between devices without storing data on a central server.

faqs.question: What platforms does Syncthing support?
faqs.answer: Syncthing runs on macOS, Windows, Linux, FreeBSD, Solaris, OpenBSD, and other platforms, on both desktops and servers.

faqs.question: Is commercial support available for Syncthing?
faqs.answer: Yes, optional commercial support is available through Kastelo, a third party.

use_cases.title: Cross-device personal file sync
use_cases.description: Keeping files synchronized across a person's own laptop, desktop, and server without a cloud provider.

use_cases.title: Trusted team file sharing
use_cases.description: Sharing folders directly between trusted collaborators' devices without a central storage provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Syncthing?
paragraphs: Syncthing is a free, open-source continuous file synchronization program that keeps files in sync across multiple devices in real time. It uses an open, published protocol and encrypts all communication with TLS and perfect forward secrecy, authenticating devices via cryptographic certificates.
Unlike cloud-sync services, Syncthing has no central server — data is stored only on the user's own devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Syncthing is for
paragraphs: Syncthing is built for users who want to keep files synchronized between their own devices, or with trusted collaborators, while keeping full control over where data is stored.

enrichment.short_description: Peer-to-peer file synchronization app for individuals that syncs files across devices without cloud storage.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Resilio Sync is a free, peer-to-peer file synchronization app that syncs photos, videos, and documents across devices without cloud storage.

features.title: Automatic folder synchronization
features.description: Keeps folders in sync automatically across multiple devices using peer-to-peer transfer.

features.title: Selective Sync
features.description: Lets users download only the files they need instead of replicating an entire folder.

features.title: Advanced Folders
features.description: Allows modifying access permissions, assigning ownership, and revoking access to shared folders.

features.title: Large file sharing
features.description: Share large files with others without maintaining a permanent folder connection.

features.title: Bandwidth control
features.description: Lets users manage network usage during sync operations.

features.title: Cloud-free operation
features.description: Uses distributed peer-to-peer technology instead of routing files through a cloud server.

pros: Free download now includes features that were previously Pro-only
No cloud storage required — files transfer directly between devices
Selective sync avoids downloading unneeded files
Granular folder permissions and access revocation
cons: Requires devices to be reachable (online) for peer-to-peer transfer
Now part of Nasuni, an enterprise-focused parent company, which may shift product priorities
Full current platform list not detailed on the individuals page

faqs.question: Is Resilio Sync free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Resilio's free Sync download now includes all the premium features that were previously only available in the paid Sync Pro plan.

faqs.question: Does Resilio Sync use the cloud?
faqs.answer: No, Resilio Sync uses peer-to-peer technology to transfer files directly between devices rather than storing them in the cloud.

faqs.question: Who owns Resilio Sync?
faqs.answer: Resilio is now part of Nasuni, a company focused on unstructured data infrastructure for enterprises.

faqs.question: Can I control who accesses a synced folder?
faqs.answer: Yes, Advanced Folders let you modify permissions, assign ownership, and revoke access to shared folders.

use_cases.title: Personal multi-device sync
use_cases.description: Keeping photos, videos, and documents in sync across a person's own devices without a cloud subscription.

use_cases.title: Family file sharing
use_cases.description: Sharing large personal files with family members via direct peer-to-peer transfer.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Resilio Sync?
paragraphs: Resilio Sync is a personal file synchronization and sharing tool that uses peer-to-peer technology to sync photos, videos, music, and documents across devices without routing them through the cloud.
The free download now includes features such as Selective Sync and Advanced Folders that were previously reserved for a paid Sync Pro plan. Resilio is now part of Nasuni.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Resilio Sync is for
paragraphs: Resilio Sync is aimed at individuals and families who want to keep files synchronized across their own devices while prioritizing privacy and avoiding cloud storage subscriptions.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source bidirectional file synchronization tool that keeps two file replicas in sync across platforms.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Unison is a free, open-source GPL-licensed tool for bidirectional file synchronization between two replicas across OSX, Unix, and Windows.

features.title: Bidirectional synchronization
features.description: Unlike simple mirroring, Unison can handle updates made to either of two replicas, not just one direction.

features.title: Cross-platform sync
features.description: Synchronizes between OSX, Unix (Solaris, Linux), and Windows systems.

features.title: Conflict detection
features.description: Detects conflicting changes between replicas and lets the user resolve them.

features.title: SSH or socket transport
features.description: Works over the internet using direct sockets or encrypted SSH connections.

features.title: Bandwidth-efficient transfer
features.description: Optimized to transfer small updates efficiently even for large files, and to run well over slow links.

features.title: Failure resilience
features.description: Designed to recover cleanly from network failures or abnormal termination.

pros: Free and open source under the GNU Public License
True bidirectional sync, handling changes on either replica
Works across mixed OS environments (OSX, Unix, Windows)
Can sync securely over SSH
cons: Maintained by a very small volunteer team with limited resources
No graphical polish or modern UI compared to commercial sync tools
Older, unmaintained releases are explicitly unsupported — users must run the latest release or a recent git build

faqs.question: Is Unison still maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, though by a small team; the project continues to publish releases (e.g. version 2.53.1) via its GitHub repository.

faqs.question: Is Unison free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Unison is free software with full source code available under the GNU Public License.

faqs.question: What platforms does Unison run on?
faqs.answer: Unison runs on OSX, Unix (including Solaris and Linux), and Windows, and can sync between different operating systems.

faqs.question: How does Unison differ from a backup tool?
faqs.answer: Unison performs bidirectional synchronization, propagating updates made to either replica, unlike simple mirroring or one-way backup utilities.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform replica sync
use_cases.description: Keeping a folder in sync between machines running different operating systems.

use_cases.title: Secure sync over SSH
use_cases.description: Synchronizing files between a local machine and a remote server over an encrypted connection.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Unison?
paragraphs: Unison is a free, open-source file synchronization tool that keeps two copies of a set of files and directories in sync, propagating changes in both directions and detecting conflicting updates. It is licensed under the GNU Public License.
It runs over direct sockets or encrypted SSH connections and is designed to work efficiently over slow network links and recover cleanly from interruptions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Unison is for
paragraphs: Unison suits users who need to keep two replicas of files synchronized across different operating systems, such as OSX, Unix, and Windows, particularly over SSH.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source command-line tool for syncing, copying, and mounting files across 70+ cloud storage providers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rclone is a free, open-source MIT-licensed command-line tool for syncing and managing files across 70+ cloud storage providers.

features.title: Sync, copy, and move commands
features.description: Syncs, copies, moves, and deletes files across cloud storage services from the command line.

features.title: Bisync bidirectional sync
features.description: Supports bidirectional synchronization between two locations via its Bisync feature.

features.title: Filesystem mounting
features.description: Mounts cloud storage as a local filesystem on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD.

features.title: Server-side transfers
features.description: Uses server-side transfer between supported providers to minimize bandwidth usage.

features.title: Virtual backends
features.description: Provides encryption, compression, and chunking through composable virtual backends.

features.title: Multi-protocol serving
features.description: Can serve data over SFTP, HTTP, WebDAV, FTP, and DLNA.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Supports over 70 cloud storage providers plus standard protocols
Checksums and timestamp preservation protect data integrity during transfer
Actively developed with a large GitHub community (58,000+ stars)
cons: Command-line only; no official first-party GUI
Requires familiarity with terminal usage and remote configuration syntax
No official paid support tier for enterprises

faqs.question: Is Rclone free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rclone is free, open-source software licensed under the MIT license.

faqs.question: How many cloud providers does Rclone support?
faqs.answer: Rclone supports over 70 cloud storage providers, including Amazon S3, Google Drive, Dropbox, Microsoft OneDrive, Backblaze B2, and Azure Blob Storage, plus protocols like WebDAV, FTP, and SFTP.

faqs.question: Can Rclone mount cloud storage as a local drive?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rclone can mount remote storage as a local filesystem on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD.

faqs.question: Does Rclone support bidirectional sync?
faqs.answer: Yes, through its Bisync feature.

use_cases.title: Cloud-to-cloud migration
use_cases.description: Moving or syncing large volumes of data directly between cloud storage providers.

use_cases.title: Automated cloud backup
use_cases.description: Scripting scheduled backups and restores to cloud storage from servers or workstations.

use_cases.title: Multi-account cloud management
use_cases.description: Managing files across several personal or business cloud storage accounts from one CLI tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Rclone?
paragraphs: Rclone is a free, open-source command-line program for managing files across cloud storage platforms, often described as 'the Swiss army knife of cloud storage.' It supports syncing, copying, moving, and deleting files, plus bidirectional sync via Bisync.
It can mount cloud storage as a local filesystem, perform server-side transfers to save bandwidth, and serve data over protocols like SFTP, HTTP, WebDAV, and FTP.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Rclone is for
paragraphs: Rclone is built for developers, system administrators, and anyone comfortable with the command line who needs to move, sync, or back up data across more than 70 supported cloud storage providers.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source file synchronization and network forwarding tool for connecting local development tools to remote infrastructure.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mutagen is free, open-source MIT-licensed file synchronization and network forwarding software for local-to-remote developer workflows, now part of Docker.

features.title: Two-way file synchronization
features.description: Fast, real-time two-way code sync using a custom algorithm with low-latency filesystem watching.

features.title: Network forwarding
features.description: Forwards TCP traffic, Unix domain sockets, and Windows named pipes between local and remote endpoints.

features.title: Docker and Compose integration
features.description: Connects directly to Docker containers and integrates with Docker Compose projects via Mutagen Compose.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Handles multiple operating systems with automatic platform-specific adjustments.

features.title: SSH and local transports
features.description: Synchronizes over local, SSH, or Docker container transports.

pros: Core code is open source under the MIT license
Fast two-way sync well suited to remote and containerized development
Deep Docker Compose integration via Mutagen Compose
Backed by Docker following its 2023 acquisition, with no planned licensing changes to open-source components
cons: Mutagen Pro paid subscriptions are no longer offered
The original Docker Desktop Mutagen extension has been replaced by Docker's own Synchronized File Shares feature (requiring a paid Docker subscription)
Some newer release binaries bundle SSPL-licensed code alongside the MIT core

faqs.question: Is Mutagen free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mutagen's core is open source under the MIT license. Mutagen Pro paid subscriptions are no longer being offered.

faqs.question: Who owns Mutagen?
faqs.answer: Docker acquired Mutagen IO, Inc. in 2023 to invest in Docker Desktop, with no planned changes to Mutagen's open-source licensing.

faqs.question: Does Mutagen work with Docker Compose?
faqs.answer: Yes, via the separate Mutagen Compose project, which uses x-mutagen extension fields in Compose YAML files.

faqs.question: What does Mutagen sync besides files?
faqs.answer: It also forwards network traffic — TCP sockets, Unix domain sockets, and Windows named pipes — between local and remote endpoints.

use_cases.title: Remote development file sync
use_cases.description: Keeping a local codebase in sync with a remote server or cloud development environment in real time.

use_cases.title: Docker Compose development
use_cases.description: Speeding up file sync into Docker containers during local development using Mutagen Compose.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Mutagen?
paragraphs: Mutagen is an open-source tool, originally founded in 2016, that provides fast two-way file synchronization and network forwarding for connecting local development tools to remote or containerized infrastructure. Its core code is MIT-licensed.

Docker acquired Mutagen IO, Inc. in 2023 to strengthen Docker Desktop. Mutagen Pro paid subscriptions have since been discontinued, and the original Docker Desktop Mutagen extension was replaced by Docker's own Synchronized File Shares feature.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Mutagen is for
paragraphs: Mutagen is aimed at developers who work against remote servers, cloud environments, or Docker containers and want their local editor and tools to stay in sync with that remote code in near real time.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source offline reader that lets you download and browse Wikipedia and other web content without an internet connection.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kiwix is a free, open-source offline reader for browsing Wikipedia and other web content without internet access, available on desktop and mobile.

features.title: Offline content reader
features.description: Kiwix Reader opens ZIM-format archives so users can browse Wikipedia, Wiktionary, StackExchange, Gutenberg and other sites with no internet connection.

features.title: Kiwix Server
features.description: A self-hosted server component (kiwix-serve) lets organizations share offline content archives over a local network.

features.title: Kiwix Hotspot
features.description: Turns a device into a local wifi hotspot to distribute offline content to nearby users without any internet infrastructure.

features.title: ZIM content catalog
features.description: A built-in catalog and imager let users browse and download curated ZIM archives of educational and reference content.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Native apps and packages are available for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android and iOS, plus Chrome, Firefox and Edge browser extensions.

pros: Completely free and open source (CC-BY-SA)
Works fully offline once content is downloaded
Available on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android and iOS
No ads and no personal data collection
Run by a non-profit with 20 years of offline-content experience
cons: Requires downloading large ZIM archive files in advance for offline use
Content freshness depends on how recently a given ZIM archive was published
Advanced deployments (Kiwix Server, Hotspot) require some technical setup

faqs.question: Is Kiwix free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kiwix is free and open-source software funded primarily through donations, released under a CC-BY-SA license.

faqs.question: What content can I access with Kiwix?
faqs.answer: Kiwix reads ZIM-format archives, which include offline copies of Wikipedia, Wiktionary, StackExchange, Project Gutenberg and other reference sites.

faqs.question: Can I use Kiwix without internet access?
faqs.answer: Yes, once you download a ZIM content file, Kiwix Reader displays it fully offline.

faqs.question: What platforms does Kiwix support?
faqs.answer: Kiwix has native applications for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android and iOS, as well as browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox and Edge.

faqs.question: Who runs Kiwix?
faqs.answer: Kiwix is developed by the Kiwix Association, a non-profit organization headquartered in Lausanne, Switzerland, founded in 2006.

use_cases.title: Education in low-connectivity regions
use_cases.description: Schools and libraries use Kiwix Hotspot and offline ZIM archives to give students access to Wikipedia and reference material without internet access.

use_cases.title: Personal offline reference library
use_cases.description: Individuals download Kiwix Reader and ZIM archives to keep a searchable offline copy of Wikipedia or other reference sites on a laptop or phone.

use_cases.title: Organizational content sharing
use_cases.description: Organizations run Kiwix Server to share offline content archives with staff over a local network without internet dependency.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Kiwix?
paragraphs: Kiwix is free, open-source software that provides offline access to knowledge. It reads ZIM-format archives, letting users browse Wikipedia and other reference content without an internet connection.

Run by the non-profit Kiwix Association based in Lausanne, Switzerland since 2006, Kiwix offers a Reader app, a self-hosted Kiwix Server, and Kiwix Hotspot for distributing offline content over local wifi. It is ad-free and does not collect personal data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Kiwix is for
paragraphs: Kiwix is used by schools, libraries and NGOs in regions with unreliable internet access, as well as individuals who want an offline reference library on their laptop, tablet or phone.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted bookmark manager designed to be minimal, fast and easy to deploy in a single Docker container.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Linkding is a free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark manager that runs in a single Docker container with automatic metadata and archiving.

features.title: Automatic metadata fetching
features.description: Linkding automatically retrieves the title, description, icon and preview image for each saved bookmark.

features.title: Website archiving
features.description: Creates local HTML snapshots of saved pages or archives them via the Internet Archive for later reference.

features.title: Bulk editing
features.description: Apply tagging, archiving or deletion actions across multiple bookmarks at once.

features.title: Import and export
features.description: Supports importing and exporting bookmarks in the standard Netscape HTML format.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Supports multiple user accounts with bookmark sharing between users.

features.title: Browser extensions
features.description: Official browser extensions for Firefox and Chrome allow saving bookmarks directly from the browser.

features.title: REST API
features.description: A REST API is available for building custom scripts and third-party integrations.

pros: Free and open source
Deploys as a single Docker container with SQLite, minimal operational overhead
Automatic metadata fetching and website archiving
Official Firefox and Chrome browser extensions
REST API for custom integrations
cons: Requires self-hosting; no official managed cloud version
No native mobile apps, relies on browser extensions and the web UI
Users are responsible for their own backups and updates

faqs.question: Is Linkding free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Linkding is free, open-source software that you self-host.

faqs.question: How do I install Linkding?
faqs.answer: Linkding is designed to run in a single Docker container using SQLite as its database.

faqs.question: Does Linkding have browser extensions?
faqs.answer: Yes, official extensions are available for Firefox and Chrome.

faqs.question: Can I import my existing bookmarks?
faqs.answer: Yes, Linkding supports importing and exporting bookmarks in the Netscape HTML bookmark format.

faqs.question: Does Linkding have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a REST API is provided for building custom scripts and integrations.

use_cases.title: Personal bookmark archive
use_cases.description: Individuals self-host Linkding to keep a private, searchable archive of saved links with automatic snapshots.

use_cases.title: Shared team bookmarks
use_cases.description: Teams run a shared Linkding instance with multiple user accounts to collect and organize useful links.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Linkding?
paragraphs: Linkding is a self-hosted bookmark manager built to be minimal, fast and easy to set up. It runs in a single Docker container using SQLite, and automatically fetches titles, descriptions, icons and preview images for saved links.

It supports website archiving, bulk bookmark editing, Netscape HTML import/export, multi-user accounts, and official browser extensions for Firefox and Chrome, alongside a REST API for custom integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Linkding is for
paragraphs: Linkding suits self-hosters and privacy-conscious users who want a lightweight, low-maintenance bookmark manager they fully control, rather than a hosted third-party service.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source bookmark archive with tagging, Internet Archive backups, and a full REST API.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LinkAce is a free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark archive with automatic Internet Archive backups, tagging and a full REST API.

features.title: Automatic Internet Archive backups
features.description: LinkAce can automatically back up saved links to the Internet Archive to protect against link rot.

features.title: Link monitoring
features.description: Monitors saved links to detect when they go offline or change.

features.title: Full REST API
features.description: Exposes all features via a REST API, allowing control and access to data from other applications.

features.title: Zapier integration
features.description: Connects to more than 5,000 other apps such as Google Drive, Discord and Slack through Zapier.

features.title: Browser bookmarklet
features.description: A bookmarklet allows quickly saving links from any browser.

features.title: Advanced search and filtering
features.description: Supports advanced search with multiple filters and ordering options, plus HTML bookmark import/export.

features.title: S3-compatible backups
features.description: Supports complete database and application backups to any AWS S3-compatible storage.

pros: Free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license
Automatic Internet Archive backups reduce link rot
Full REST API and Zapier integration for automation
Supports multiple languages including English, German, French, Japanese, Spanish, Polish and Chinese
Docker-based installer simplifies self-hosted setup
cons: Requires self-hosting; no official managed cloud version
Setup and maintenance require basic server/Docker knowledge

faqs.question: Is LinkAce free?
faqs.answer: Yes, LinkAce is free and open source, released under the GPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: How do I install LinkAce?
faqs.answer: LinkAce ships with pre-configured Docker setups and a built-in web installer; Docker is the recommended installation method.

faqs.question: Does LinkAce back up links automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, LinkAce can automatically back up saved links to the Internet Archive and monitors them for availability changes.

faqs.question: Does LinkAce have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a full REST API exposes all features for use in other applications.

faqs.question: What languages does LinkAce support?
faqs.answer: LinkAce supports multiple languages, including English, German, French, Japanese, Spanish, Polish and Chinese.

use_cases.title: Personal permanent link library
use_cases.description: Individuals self-host LinkAce to build a permanent, searchable archive of bookmarks with automatic Internet Archive backups.

use_cases.title: Automated bookmark workflows
use_cases.description: Users connect LinkAce to other apps via its REST API and Zapier integration to automate saving and organizing links.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is LinkAce?
paragraphs: LinkAce is a free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark archive. It automatically backs up saved links to the Internet Archive, monitors links for availability, and offers advanced search and filtering.

It provides a full REST API, a Zapier integration connecting to more than 5,000 apps, a browser bookmarklet, HTML import/export, and support for backing up its database to S3-compatible storage. It is released under the GPL-3.0 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who LinkAce is for
paragraphs: LinkAce suits self-hosters who want a permanent, archive-backed bookmark library with automation options via API and Zapier, rather than a hosted third-party bookmarking service.

enrichment.short_description: Minimalist, self-hosted bookmark manager and link-sharing service that stores links in files with no database required.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Shaarli is a free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark manager and link-sharing tool that stores links in files with no database required.

features.title: File-based storage
features.description: Shaarli stores saved links (shaares) in files, so no database installation is required.

features.title: Editable link metadata
features.description: Each saved link can be edited with a title, description, tags, and privacy status.

features.title: Tag cloud and picture wall views
features.description: Browse saved links through a tag cloud/list view or a visual picture wall.

features.title: RSS and ATOM feeds
features.description: Generates ATOM and RSS feeds that can be filtered by tags, including a daily digest feed.

features.title: Automatic URL cleanup
features.description: Automatically strips tracking parameters from saved URLs.

features.title: Plugin system and REST API
features.description: Extensible via plugins and a REST API for building third-party client applications.

features.title: LDAP login support
features.description: Supports LDAP authentication for login.

pros: Free and open source
No database required, simplifying installation and backups
Lightweight and minimalist, easy to drop onto a server
Plugin system and REST API for extensibility
Docker deployment supported alongside traditional server install
cons: Requires self-hosting; no official managed cloud version
Minimalist feature set compared to more full-featured bookmarking apps
No native mobile apps beyond the responsive web interface

faqs.question: Is Shaarli free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Shaarli is free, open-source, self-hosted software.

faqs.question: Does Shaarli require a database?
faqs.answer: No, Shaarli stores saved links (shaares) in files instead of requiring a database.

faqs.question: Can I install Shaarli with Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, Shaarli supports both traditional server installation and Docker deployment.

faqs.question: Does Shaarli support RSS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Shaarli generates ATOM and RSS feeds that can be filtered by tags.

faqs.question: Can Shaarli be extended with plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, Shaarli has a plugin system and a REST API for building integrations.

use_cases.title: Lightweight personal bookmarking
use_cases.description: Individuals self-host Shaarli as a simple, file-based bookmark manager without database overhead.

use_cases.title: Public link sharing and microblogging
use_cases.description: Users publish curated public link lists or short notes via Shaarli's link-sharing and daily digest features.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Shaarli?
paragraphs: Shaarli is a minimalist bookmark manager and link-sharing service that you install on your own server. It supports use cases including link sharing, bookmarking, microblogging, read-it-later lists, note-taking and knowledge bases.

It stores saved links in files rather than a database, offers tag cloud and picture wall views, ATOM/RSS feeds filterable by tags, automatic tracking-parameter cleanup, a plugin system, and a REST API. It is a community-maintained fork of the original project by Sébastien Sauvage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Shaarli is for
paragraphs: Shaarli suits self-hosters who want a lightweight, database-free bookmarking and link-sharing tool, including bloggers who publish curated public link lists.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source read-it-later app that saves readable copies of web articles, images and videos for offline keeping.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Readeck is a free, open-source, self-hosted read-it-later app built in Go that saves readable web articles for offline access.

features.title: Readable content extraction
features.description: Saves the readable content of web pages, including articles, images and videos, for permanent offline keeping.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Searches across all saved content to quickly find previously bookmarked material.

features.title: Labels, favorites and archives
features.description: Organizes saved items with labels, favorites and an archive for long-term storage.

features.title: Highlights and annotations
features.description: Lets users highlight and annotate saved articles.

features.title: Collections
features.description: Saves custom search queries as collections for quick access later.

features.title: EPUB export and OPDS catalog
features.description: Exports saved articles as EPUB e-books and exposes an OPDS catalog for e-reader apps.

features.title: Browser extensions
features.description: Firefox and Chrome browser extensions let users save pages directly while browsing.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license
Simple self-hosted deployment via a single Docker/Podman container or standalone binary
EPUB export and OPDS support for e-reader users
Lightweight Go backend using server-side rendering instead of a heavy SPA
Browser extensions for Firefox and Chrome
cons: Requires self-hosting; no official managed cloud version
Newer project with a smaller community than more established read-it-later tools

faqs.question: Is Readeck free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Readeck is free, open-source software released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: How do I self-host Readeck?
faqs.answer: Readeck can be deployed as a single Docker or Podman container, as a standalone binary, or built from source, typically using SQLite as the database.

faqs.question: Can I export saved articles as e-books?
faqs.answer: Yes, Readeck supports exporting saved content as EPUB files and offers an OPDS catalog for e-readers.

faqs.question: Does Readeck have browser extensions?
faqs.answer: Yes, Firefox and Chrome browser extensions are available for saving pages while browsing.

faqs.question: What is Readeck built with?
faqs.answer: Readeck's backend is built primarily in Go, with server-side rendering using Stimulus and Turbo.

use_cases.title: Personal offline reading archive
use_cases.description: Individuals self-host Readeck to save articles, images and videos for distraction-free, permanent offline reading.

use_cases.title: E-reader article syncing
use_cases.description: Users export saved articles as EPUB or connect an e-reader via OPDS to read saved content on dedicated devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Readeck?
paragraphs: Readeck is a free, open-source, self-hosted read-it-later application. It saves the readable content of web pages, including articles, images and videos, for permanent offline keeping.

Built in Go with server-side rendering, Readeck offers full-text search, labels, favorites, archives, highlights, collections, EPUB export, an OPDS catalog for e-readers, and browser extensions for Firefox and Chrome. It is released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Readeck is for
paragraphs: Readeck suits self-hosters who want a lightweight, privacy-respecting read-it-later tool, particularly those who read saved articles on e-readers via EPUB or OPDS.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted read-it-later app for saving, highlighting and annotating articles, now available only as a self-hosted server.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Omnivore is a free, open-source read-it-later app for saving and highlighting articles, now self-hosted only after its cloud service was deprecated.

features.title: Highlighting and notes
features.description: Users can highlight passages and add notes to saved articles.

features.title: Full-text search and sharing
features.description: Searches across all saved content and supports sharing saved articles.

features.title: Native mobile apps and PWA
features.description: Includes a native iOS app, an Android app, and a progressive web app for offline reading.

features.title: Browser extensions
features.description: Extensions are available for Chrome, Safari, Firefox and Edge to save pages while browsing.

features.title: PDF support
features.description: Supports saving and reading PDF documents alongside web articles.

features.title: Newsletter integration
features.description: Can receive and store newsletter content, including Substack support.

features.title: Logseq and Obsidian integration
features.description: Offers integration plugins for exporting highlights and notes to Logseq and Obsidian.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license
Full self-hosting guide available for running your own server
Native iOS and Android apps plus a progressive web app
Browser extensions for Chrome, Safari, Firefox and Edge
Integrations with Logseq and Obsidian for note-taking workflows
cons: The hosted cloud application was deprecated in November 2024, so it now requires self-hosting
Self-hosting requires setting up and maintaining your own server infrastructure
Text-to-speech is limited to iOS

faqs.question: Is Omnivore still available as a hosted service?
faqs.answer: No, Omnivore's cloud application was deprecated in November 2024. It is now a self-hosted-only application.

faqs.question: Is Omnivore free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Omnivore is free, open-source software released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Does Omnivore have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has a native iOS app, an Android app, and a progressive web app.

faqs.question: Can I save newsletters in Omnivore?
faqs.answer: Yes, Omnivore supports newsletter integration, including Substack.

faqs.question: Does Omnivore integrate with note-taking apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers integration plugins for Logseq and Obsidian.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted read-it-later archive
use_cases.description: Technical users self-host Omnivore to save, highlight and annotate articles without relying on a third-party cloud service.

use_cases.title: Note-taking workflow integration
use_cases.description: Users export highlights and notes from Omnivore into Logseq or Obsidian as part of a personal knowledge management workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Omnivore?
paragraphs: Omnivore is a complete, open-source read-it-later solution for saving, highlighting, annotating and searching articles. Its cloud-hosted application was deprecated in November 2024, and it is now available only as a self-hosted application.

It offers native iOS and Android apps, a progressive web app, browser extensions for Chrome, Safari, Firefox and Edge, PDF support, newsletter integration including Substack, and integrations with Logseq and Obsidian. It is released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Omnivore is for
paragraphs: Omnivore suits technically inclined readers who want a full-featured, self-hosted read-it-later tool and are comfortable running their own server since the cloud version is no longer available.

enrichment.short_description: Nonprofit suite of open-source web archiving tools, including Browsertrix, ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page, for capturing and replaying web pages.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Webrecorder builds open-source web archiving tools, including Browsertrix, ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page, for capturing and replaying web pages.

features.title: Browsertrix crawling platform
features.description: An automated, browser-based crawling platform for creating interactive web archives at scale, offered with free trials and tiered plans.

features.title: ArchiveWeb.page
features.description: A free Chrome extension and desktop app for recording complex, interactive web page behavior while browsing.

features.title: ReplayWeb.page
features.description: A free, serverless playback tool for viewing archived WARC/WACZ files directly in the browser.

features.title: Login-protected page capture
features.description: Can archive pages that require a login, along with interactive and social media content.

features.title: Deduplication
features.description: Uses deduplication technology to reduce storage requirements for large web archives.

features.title: Open-source developer tools
features.description: Provides open-source libraries, command-line tools and file format specifications on GitHub.

pros: ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page are free to use
Handles complex, interactive and login-protected web content that simple crawlers miss
Run by a nonprofit focused on digital preservation, used by libraries and universities
Deduplication reduces storage costs for large-scale archives
Open-source tools and file format specs available on GitHub
cons: Browsertrix's full crawling platform is a paid, tiered service beyond the free trial
Large-scale web archiving still requires technical setup and storage planning

faqs.question: Is Webrecorder free?
faqs.answer: Some Webrecorder tools, including ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page, are free. Browsertrix, its automated crawling platform, offers a free trial with paid tiered plans.

faqs.question: What is Browsertrix?
faqs.answer: Browsertrix is Webrecorder's automated, browser-based crawling platform for creating interactive web archives at scale.

faqs.question: Can Webrecorder archive login-protected pages?
faqs.answer: Yes, its tools are built to capture login-protected pages and interactive web content, including social media posts.

faqs.question: Who uses Webrecorder?
faqs.answer: Webrecorder is used by libraries, universities, governments and cultural organizations for digital preservation.

faqs.question: Is Webrecorder open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Webrecorder's developer tools, libraries and file format specifications are open source and available on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Institutional web archiving
use_cases.description: Libraries and universities use Browsertrix to crawl and preserve interactive websites at scale for long-term digital archives.

use_cases.title: Personal page capture
use_cases.description: Individuals use the free ArchiveWeb.page extension to capture complex or interactive pages they want to preserve.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Webrecorder?
paragraphs: Webrecorder is a nonprofit that builds open-source tools for web archiving and digital preservation. Its suite includes Browsertrix, an automated browser-based crawling platform; ArchiveWeb.page, a free Chrome extension and desktop app for recording interactive browsing sessions; and ReplayWeb.page, a free serverless tool for replaying WARC/WACZ archives.
The tools are built to capture complex, interactive and login-protected web content, including social media, and use deduplication technology to reduce storage needs for large archives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Webrecorder is for
paragraphs: Webrecorder is used by libraries, universities, governments and cultural institutions doing large-scale digital preservation, as well as individuals and researchers who need to capture interactive web pages.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted tiling image board for saving, tagging and sharing images, videos and web pages.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pinry is a free, open-source, self-hosted image board for saving, tagging and sharing images, videos and web pages.

features.title: Tagging system
features.description: Users can assign multiple tags to every pin and instantly filter their entire collection by tag.

features.title: Public and private boards
features.description: Supports both public boards for sharing and private boards for personal collections.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Allows multiple user accounts to save and manage their own pins.

features.title: REST API
features.description: A full REST API built on Django REST Framework exposes pin and board data for integrations.

features.title: Single-container Docker deployment
features.description: Ships as a single Docker container backed by SQLite for a zero-dependency setup.

features.title: Browser extension compatibility
features.description: Works with browser extensions for saving images and pages directly to Pinry.

pros: Free and open source under the BSD-2-Clause license
Simple self-hosted deployment as a single Docker container with SQLite
Tag-based organization with instant filtering
Full REST API for integrations
No ads and no account required for public browsing if enabled
cons: Requires self-hosting; no official managed cloud version
Simpler feature set than commercial visual bookmarking tools like Pinterest

faqs.question: Is Pinry free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pinry is free, open-source software released under the BSD-2-Clause license.

faqs.question: How do I self-host Pinry?
faqs.answer: Pinry ships as a single Docker container backed by SQLite, designed for a zero-dependency self-hosted setup.

faqs.question: What is Pinry built with?
faqs.answer: Pinry combines a Django REST Framework backend with a Vue.js frontend.

faqs.question: Can multiple people use the same Pinry instance?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pinry supports multiple user accounts with public and private boards.

faqs.question: Does Pinry have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pinry provides a full REST API for accessing and managing pins and boards.

use_cases.title: Personal visual bookmarking
use_cases.description: Individuals self-host Pinry to save and tag images, videos and web pages in a private, ad-free tiling board.

use_cases.title: Team image collections
use_cases.description: Small teams, such as photographers or designers, use Pinry's public/private boards to collaborate on shared visual references.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Pinry?
paragraphs: Pinry is a free, open-source, self-hosted tiling image board for saving, tagging and sharing images, videos and web pages in an easy-to-skim format.

Built with a Django REST Framework backend and a Vue.js frontend, Pinry ships as a single Docker container with SQLite. It supports multi-user accounts, public and private boards, tag-based filtering, and a full REST API, and is released under the BSD-2-Clause license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Pinry is for
paragraphs: Pinry suits self-hosters, photographers and small teams who want a private, ad-free alternative to hosted visual bookmarking services for collecting and organizing images.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted photo and video backup application with mobile auto-backup, built as an open-source alternative to cloud photo storage.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Immich is a free, open-source, self-hosted photo and video backup app with mobile auto-backup, built by FUTO under the AGPL v3 license.

features.title: Mobile auto-backup
features.description: The mobile app selectively backs up photos and videos from iOS and Android devices to your own Immich server.

features.title: Web interface
features.description: A browser-based interface for viewing, managing and organizing your media library.

features.title: Facial clusters
features.description: Groups photos by detected faces to help organize a media library, with documentation guidance for improving cluster accuracy.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: An admin user can create and manage additional user accounts on the same server.

features.title: Database backup tools
features.description: Includes built-in capabilities for backing up metadata and user information.

features.title: Docker-based self-hosting
features.description: Deploys via Docker containers, using PostgreSQL as its database, on your own infrastructure.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPL v3 license
Mobile apps available on the Apple App Store, Google Play, and F-Droid
Full control over storage location and data since it's self-hosted
Facial clustering helps organize large photo libraries
Active development backed by FUTO with a public roadmap
cons: Requires self-hosting via Docker; no official managed cloud version
Recommended minimum of 6GB RAM and 2 CPU cores for the server
Users are responsible for their own server maintenance, backups and updates

faqs.question: Is Immich free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Immich is free, open-source software released under the AGPL v3 license.

faqs.question: What are the server requirements for Immich?
faqs.answer: Immich recommends a minimum of 6GB RAM and 2 CPU cores, Docker, and a PostgreSQL database.

faqs.question: Does Immich have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, Immich has mobile apps available on the Apple App Store, Google Play Store, and F-Droid with automatic backup.

faqs.question: Who develops Immich?
faqs.answer: Immich is developed by FUTO.

faqs.question: Can multiple people use one Immich server?
faqs.answer: Yes, an admin user can add and manage additional user accounts on the same Immich instance.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted photo backup
use_cases.description: Individuals run Immich on their own server or NAS to automatically back up phone photos and videos instead of using a commercial cloud photo service.

use_cases.title: Family photo library
use_cases.description: Families use Immich's multi-user support to give several people accounts on a shared, self-hosted photo library.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Immich?
paragraphs: Immich is a self-hosted photo and video backup application that lets users manage their personal media library on their own infrastructure instead of relying on a commercial cloud service.

It offers mobile apps with automatic backup for iOS and Android, a web interface, facial clustering, multi-user support, and Docker-based deployment with PostgreSQL. It is free and open source under the AGPL v3 license, developed by FUTO.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Immich is for
paragraphs: Immich suits self-hosters, privacy-conscious individuals, and families who want a Google Photos-style auto-backup experience while keeping their photo and video library on infrastructure they control.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is PhotoPrism?
paragraphs: PhotoPrism is an AI-powered, privacy-first application for browsing, organizing, and sharing photos and videos. It is built and maintained by PhotoPrism UG, a self-funded, independent company based in Berlin, Germany, founded in 2018, and is designed to be self-hosted so users retain control over their media and data.

The Community Edition is free and open source under the AGPL 3.0 license, offering unlimited storage and instances for personal use. Paid Essentials and Plus editions add features such as additional user roles, enhanced geocoding, and 3D vector and satellite maps, while PikaPods offers fully managed hosting starting from $6.50 per month for users who prefer not to self-host.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who PhotoPrism is for
paragraphs: PhotoPrism is aimed at privacy-conscious individuals, families, and small teams who want an alternative to commercial cloud photo services without giving up AI-powered features like face recognition and automatic content classification.

Because it supports RAW image formats, multiple video formats, and deployment via Docker on hardware ranging from Raspberry Pi to NAS devices, it also suits home-lab and self-hosting enthusiasts who want control over their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is LibrePhotos?
paragraphs: LibrePhotos is a self-hosted, open-source photo management service with automatic face recognition, object detection, and semantic search, powered by machine learning models such as face_recognition, scikit-learn, and places365. It is released under the MIT license and is actively maintained, with version 1.0.3 released in June 2026.

The application supports all major photo types, including RAW images and videos, and organizes them into a timeline view with event-based albums. It uses a Django and PostgreSQL backend with a React/TypeScript frontend, and is deployed via Docker and Docker Compose.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who LibrePhotos is for
paragraphs: LibrePhotos is built for individuals and families who want commercial-grade AI photo organization features, such as face recognition and semantic search, without sending their images to a third-party cloud service.
Its multiuser support and dedicated Android client also make it suitable for households or small groups sharing a self-hosted photo library.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Piwigo?
paragraphs: Piwigo is open-source photo gallery software for organizing, managing, and sharing photos on the web, in continuous development since 2002. It is released under the GPL v2 license and can be self-hosted on a PHP/MySQL server, or used through piwigo.com's managed cloud hosting plans.

The software supports album hierarchies, batch photo management, and granular permissions, and can be extended with more than 200 community plugins and themes. It is available in a dozen or more languages, including English, French, German, and Spanish.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Piwigo is for
paragraphs: Piwigo suits organizations, teams, and individuals who need to manage large photo libraries, from thousands to hundreds of thousands of images, with fine-grained permission controls.
Its self-hosted option appeals to users comfortable managing their own PHP/MySQL server, while its piwigo.com cloud plans, which include a 30-day free trial, serve users who prefer managed hosting.

It uses a dual-licensing model: a free edition released under the AGPLv3 open-source license, and paid Lite and Pro editions under a proprietary commercial license that add features like advanced storage integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Chevereto is for
